---
output: 
  phstemplates::phs_report_docx:
    reference_docx: "phs-offdev-report.docx"
    fig_caption: no
    cover_page: "phs-offdev-cover.docx"
    cover_title: "My title"
    cover_subtitle: "My subtitle"
    cover_date: "DD MM YYYY"
    toc_depth: 3
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}

# CAMHS

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, dpi = 300)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(magrittr)
library(arrow)
library(tidyverse)

# define dataset selector variable
dataset_choice <- "CAMHS"

# source data for tables and inline values
source("/PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/scripts/luke/CAPTND/07_publication/script/functions/source_data_for_markdown.R", local = knitr::knit_global())

```

# Introduction

This publication of statistics in development presents information relating to 
appointment activity in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS)
from the Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND), 
at NHS board and Scotland-level. Presented in this report are data on appointment 
Did Not Attend (DNA) rates amongst CAMHS patients relating to the period between 
April 2025 and June 2026. Total appointment and first contact appointment DNA 
rates are presented by population group to try and better understand patterns of 
non-attendance across different demographics, and determine whether differences 
in DNA rates represent inequalities in access to PT services across NHS Scotland. 
Here ‘Scotland-level’ refers to the aggregated regional NHS board figures that 
are available in CAPTND.  

CAPTND is a dataset in development that collates information for mental health 
services at patient-level and records each individual’s journey through the service. 
CAPTND data are still under development therefore any inferences or 
conclusions from this analysis must be treated with caution, and should not be compared 
to the [official aggregate CAMHS collection and waiting times](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-and-adolescent-mental-health-services-camhs-waiting-times/child-and-adolescent-mental-health-services-camhs-waiting-times-quarter-ending-september-2025/).  

## Data Quality

Good quality data are crucial for accuracy and interpretation, and Public Health 
Scotland is working with all NHS boards to implement robust data collection for 
CAPTND. Some data items, namely ‘UCPN’, ‘UPI’, and ‘CHI’, are required to create 
a unique and complete care pathway for an individual within CAPTND (see 
[Variables Reported](#variables-reported) section below). For records where these
key variables are not available the pathway cannot be created accurately and
cannot therefore be used in CAPTND analysis. A comparison of referral counts before
and after pathway creation can be found in the accompanying [data quality document](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/child-and-adolescent-mental-health-services-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/). 

Data returns for CAPTND began in June 2019, and following extensive data quality 
checking and collaboration with NHS boards data quality improved sufficiently by 
January 2020 to enable initial and limited reporting to commence.
Improving data quality in CAPTND is an ongoing process, meaning data from previous
quarters may not be comparable to the current reporting period.   

As the development of CAPTND progresses, greater detail on individuals’ journeys 
within CAMHS will be reported.  

## Variables Reported

See the [Glossary](#glossary) for definitions of terminology and derived values.  

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Unique Care Pathway Number (UCPN):** An  identifier allocated to new referrals 
to a consultant-led, nurse-led, or other service, to enable identification of
patient pathways. These are only unique *within* NHS boards. 
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Community Health Index (CHI):** The [Community 
Health Index (CHI)](https://www.datadictionary.nhs.uk/attributes/community_health_index_number.html)
is a population register, which is used in Scotland for health care purposes. 
The CHI number uniquely identifies a person on the index, the first six digits 
of the CHI number relate to the person's date of birth (DD MM YY).
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Unique Patient Identifier (UPI):** The UPI is a locally-generated code used to
uniquely identify a patient in cases where the CHI number is not available.
</Div>
 
<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Sex:** Biological sex as recorded at birth. 'Not known' refers to indeterminate
sex at birth, including intersex. The use of 'Not specified' refers to instances
where the information is not available.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Age group:** Age in years is calculated at the point of referral from reported date 
of birth. When date of birth is not reported, the first six digits of the CHI 
number are used. Age is then grouped into 5-year age categories for reporting purposes.  
NB: CAMHS is available for children and young people aged 0-18 in Scotland (for those who meet referral criteria). In some cases, however, individuals over 18 may access CAMHS.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**SIMD quintile:** [Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation (SIMD)](https://www.gov.scot/collections/scottish-index-of-multiple-deprivation-2020/) 
is a postcode-derived measure of relative deprivation. If an area is identified as 
‘deprived’, this can relate to residents having a low income, but it can also 
mean fewer resources or opportunities. SIMD looks at the extent to which an area 
is deprived across seven domains: income, employment, education, health, access 
to services, crime and housing. Residence in a deprived postcode area does not 
necessarily mean an individual themselves experiences deprivation. Not all patients 
are treated in their NHS board of residence. Please note that NHS board areas may
not have all five quintiles represented.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Urban rural classification:** [Scottish Government Urban Rural Classification 2022](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/introduction/) 
provides a standardised approach to defining urban and rural areas, and was developed 
to improve understanding of issues facing urban, rural and remote communities in Scotland.
The classification is based on population, defined by the National Records of Scotland, 
and accessibility, based on drive time analysis to categorise accessible and 
remote areas of Scotland. The urban rural classification is available in multiple forms, 
but the 8-fold classification has been adopted for this analysis to provide greater 
granularity in comparisons between remote regions. The urban rural class applied to 
each patient is based on the postcode of their most recent referral.
</Div>    
    
<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Looked after child status:** An indication of whether the individual is *currently*
a looked after child.  
This can be:  
a) In local authority accommodation under a voluntary arrangement, where the child's
parents agree to the child being accommodated.  
b) In local authority accommodation or at home, under compulsory measures decided
by a children's hearing or a court.  
The local authority has responsibilities and duties towards a child who is being
'looked after' under the Children (Scotland) Act 1995.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Child protection status:** An indication of whether the individual is *currently*
on the Child Protection Register. Local authorities are responsible for maintaining
this confidential central register of all children and young people who are the subject
of a multi-agency Child Protection Plan. 
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral received date / Referral date:** The date on which a health or social 
care service receives a referral. If this cannot be provided, ‘Referral date’ is 
used instead as these dates are often very similar. If neither of these fields are
provided the individual’s record cannot be counted as it cannot be placed within 
the time frame reported.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral accepted:** A categorical variable stating whether an individual's
referral has been accepted, not accepted, decision pending, or the information is missing.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral non-acceptance reason:** The reason why a referral was not accepted 
by the service provider. 
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Referral non-acceptance action:** The action taken following an initial referral 
not being accepted into the service. Please note that these are data under development
and may not be recorded in all NHS boards.
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Appointment date:** The date on which an appointment occurred or was planned 
to occur. The first appointment date in an individual's pathway is referred to 
as the **first contact appointment.**
</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 
**Appointment attendance status:** Indicates whether the patient attended their 
appointment and provides context for non-attendance. This is used to calculate
Did Not Attend (DNA) rates.
</Div>


# Main Points

For CAMHS data recorded in CAPTND across NHS Scotland, in the publication period
ending `r date_label`:

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1"> 

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>

<Div custom-style = "Bullet 1">

</Div>


\newpage

# Results and Commentary
## CAMHS total appointment DNAs
Across NHS Scotland, there were a total of **261,080** appointments recorded in CAPTND 
for the publication period between April 2025 and June 2026. Data broken down by 
quarters can be seen in the accompanying [data files](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/psychological-therapies-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-1-september-2026/).     
    
The Did Not Attend (DNA) rate is an important measure to consider, as DNA appointments 
are difficult to fill due to the unplanned nature of the non-attendance 
(see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information). 
Of the **261,080** total appointments for the 15-month publication period ending June 2026, 
the overall DNA rate was **9.0%** (**23,566** appointments not attended). 
This represents a significant amount of lost capacity within CAMHS services, potentially 
impacting the length of time patients wait for treatment.   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 1: CAMHS total appointments and total DNA rate by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r Total appt DNA table, ft.align = "left"}

# Set up some formatting for flextable to be applied to most tables
my_ft_format <- function(ft) {
  ft %>%
    flextable::bold(part = "header") %>%
    bg(bg = "#43358B", part = "header") %>%
    color(color = "white", part = "header") %>%
    align(align = "left", part = "header") %>%
    valign(valign = "center", part = "header") %>%
    valign(valign = "top", part = "body") %>%
    colformat_num(big.mark = "") %>%
    font(fontname = "arial", part = "all") %>%
    fontsize(size = 11, part = "all") %>%
    border(border = fp_border_default(color = "#000000", width = 0.5),
           part = "all")
}

flextable(table_data7, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data7)) %>%
  align(j = 2:4, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:4, width = 3, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`
<br>

### CAMHS total DNA rate by age group and sex
In CAMHS, total appointment DNA rates for males and females were highest amongst
the 'Over 15' age group in NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026 at **11.5%** 
for both sexes. Amongst males, there was a stepped increase in total CAMHS appointment 
DNA rates by age, increasing from a low of **6.6%** in those aged 'Under 6' at the time 
of referral across NHS Scotland. The only aggregated age group in which total appointment
DNAs were highest amongst females (**7.7%**) compared to males (**6.6%**) was in the 
‘Under 6’ age group.

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_age_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_age_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by age group and appointment month
There appears to be some month-to-month variability in the total appointment DNA
rates recorded by aggregated age group across NHS Scotland, with similar peaks 
and troughs observed between groups suggesting a level of seasonality to rates of
non-attendances in CAMHS (Figure ). Total CAMHS appointment DNA rates were 
consistently highest amongst the 'Over 15' age group across NHS Scotland between
April 2025 and June 2026. The lowest DNA rates were generally recorded amongst the
'Under 6' age group across NHS Scotland, though monthly variation was more notable
for this group, likely due to the smaller number of CAMHS referrals received for
patients aged under 6 years old compared to the other age groups across NHS Scotland.  
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and appointment month, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_age_mth, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/dna_agg_age_tt_CAMHS.png"))

```   

### CAMHS total DNA rate by SIMD and sex

Deprivation based on SIMD quintile appears to be closely associated with total 
CAMHS appointment DNA rates across NHS Scotland. Between April 2025 and June 2026,
females from the most deprived areas (**12.2%**) were more likely to DNA a CAMHS appointment
than those from the least deprived areas (**5.9%**). A similar trend was observed amongst
male patients, with a DNA rate of **11.5%** recorded amongst those from the most deprived
SIMD quintile compared to **5.6%** in the least deprived quintile across NHS Scotland. 
DNA rates for both males and females were above the corresponding mean in both SIMD 1
(most deprived) and SIMD 2 for the publication period.

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by SIMD quintile and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, March 2025 to June 2026, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_simd_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_simd_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex
Total CAMHS appointment DNA rates for both males (**13.5%**) and females (**14.8%**) were
highest amongst patients residing in 'very remote small towns' across NHS Scotland
based on the 8-fold urban rural classification (Figure). Amongst females, total 
appointment DNA rates were lowest for patients residing in 'remote small towns' (**7.6%**),
and for males were lowest amongst patients living in 'remote rural' (**8.8%**) settings.
Given the differences in accessibility, it is worth noting that total appointment DNA
rates amongst patients residing in remote or very remote rural areas were lower the
rates recorded in large and other urban areas across NHS Scotland. This could, however,
be explained by differences in factors such as relative deprivation, and the location
of planned appointments, including the utilisation of digital technologies to deliver
therapy to more remote, rural settings. For definitions of each rurality class, 
please consult the Scottish Government’s overview [documentation](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/overview/).  
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_ur_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_ur_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by appointment location and sex
The location of the planned CAMHS appointment will vary based on the needs of the
patient and is likely to influence non-attendance rates. Figure x shows the total
appointment DNA rate by the location of the planned appointment and the patient’s
sex reported at referral. The ten most common appointment locations recorded across
NHS Scotland for the period between April 2025 and June 2026 are displayed in
descending order from top to bottom.   
    
Amongst females, total CAMHS appointment DNA rates were highest across NHS Scotland 
when the planned appointment location was recorded as 'attend anywhere' (**16.0%**). 
For males, DNA rates were highest for appointments when the planned location was 
recorded as 'day hospital' (**16.1%**) over the 15-month publication period. Total CAMHS
appointment DNAs were lowest for females when the planned appointment location was 
recorded as 'patients home' (**4.8%**), and for males when recorded as 'day centre' (**2.4%**).   
    
The difference in total appointment DNA rates between males and females was typically
quite small between planned appointment locations. However, when the planned appointment
location was recorded as 'school' or 'day centre', the DNA rate amongst females 
was notably higher compared to males across NHS Scotland for the publication period.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by location of planned appointment and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by appointment location for least deprived SIMD quintile
In the least deprived SIMD quintile, there were a total of **41,478** appointments 
offered across NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026. Figure x shows the 
total number of appointments and the DNA rate by planned appointment location for 
patients living in the least deprived SIMD quintile based on postcode at the time 
of referral. The top ten most common appointment locations recorded for patients 
living in the least deprived SIMD quintile across NHS Scotland are shown in ascending
from top to bottom.    
    
'Out-patient clinic' was the most commonly recorded appointment location over the 
period making up **58.3%** (**24,169**) of the total appointments offered, with an 
associated DNA rate of **5.7%** for the publication period. The DNA rate was below 
10.0% for all planned appointment locations with the exception of the 'day hospital',
which had a DNA rate of **11.1%** across SIMD quintile 5 in NHS Scotland.      
    
In the least deprived quintile, appointments that made use of digital technologies,
recorded as 'telephone consultations', 'NHS Near Me' and 'attend anywhere' made up
a total of **12.7%** (**5,278**) of all CAMHS appointments. The DNA rate for these appointment
types varied from **2.9%** for ‘attend anywhere’ to **9.5%** for ‘NHS Near Me’ over the 
15-month publication period in NHS Scotland.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointments and crude DNA rate for top 10 most common planned appointment locations for patients living in the least deprived SIMD quintile, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_simd5_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_simd5_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by appointment location for most deprived SIMD quintile
In the most deprived SIMD quintile, there were a total of **51,902** appointments offered
across NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026. Shown in Figure x is the 
total number of appointments offered and the DNA rate by planned appointment location
for patients living the in the most deprived SIMD quintile based on postcode at 
the time of referral. The top ten most common appointment locations recorded for 
patients living in the most deprived SIMD quintile across NHS Scotland are displayed
in ascending from top to bottom.   
    
'Out-patient clinic' was the most commonly recorded appointment location over the
period making up **40.6%** (**21,090**) of the total appointments offered, with an 
associated DNA rate of **11.0%** for the publication period – almost twice as high
as the DNA rate in the least deprived quintile.   
    
In the least deprived quintile, non face-to-face CAMHS appointments recorded with 
a location of 'telephone consultations', 'NHS Near Me' and 'attend anywhere' made up
a total of **6.5%** (**3,362**) of all appointments. This suggests that in the most 
deprived quintile, appointments utilising digital technologies made up a much 
lower proportion of total appointments offered compared to the least deprived 
quintile over the same period. The DNA rate for these appointment types varied
from **6.9%** for 'telephone consultations' to **24.1%** for 'attend anywhere' appointments
over the 15-month publication period in NHS Scotland. Also notable between the 
deprivation quintiles was the proportion of appointments with a location recorded
as 'not known'. For the least deprived SIMD quintile, the location of **22.2%** (**9,237**)
of CAMHS appointments were recorded as 'not known' compared to **43.3%** (**22,461**) of
appointments in the most deprived quintile during the publication period, 
suggesting a level of inequality in the missingness of this data item.
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointments and crude DNA rate for top 10 most common planned appointment locations for patients living in the most deprived SIMD quintile, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_loc_simd1_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_loc_simd1_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by geographic region
The total CAMHS appointment DNA rate appears to be relatively consistent across 
health board regions for the 15-month publication period, which is likely due to 
the improving data quality in CAPTND. For CAMHS, total appointment DNA rates appear
to have remained very stable at around 7% to 8% across the East region between April
2025 and June 2026. Total appointment DNA rates were highest across the North and 
West health board regions, though there does appear to have been a slight declining
trend in DNAs over the 15-month publication period. Since November 2025, total CAMHS
appointment DNAs rates have been similar across all three health board regions at 
between 8.0% and 10.0% for most months (Figure ) 
(see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information). 

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by health board region, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_hb_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/dna_hb_region_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS total DNA rate by weekday of planned appointment
There was little variation between the weekday of the planned PT appointment and 
the DNA rate across NHS Scotland for the publication period (Figure ). Total 
appointment DNA rates were, however, marginally higher on a Monday for both males
(**9.5%**) and females (**9.4%**) compared to all other weekdays between April 2025 
and June 2026.

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : Total appointment crude DNA rate by weekday planned appointment, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r tot_dnas_by_weekday_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_att/tot_dna_weekday_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```


## CAMHS first contact appointment DNAs

Of the **261,080** total appointments in CAMHS across NHS Scotland in the
latest quarter, **8.7%** (**22,826**) were first contact appointments (Table 2). 
The first contact appointment is the first appointment date a patient 
is offered in their treatment journey (see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information).  

The first contact DNA rate is represented as the proportion of all planned first
contact appointments which were not attended. DNAs in first contact appointments
represent a delay to treatment starting. The CAMHS first contact DNA rate across NHS Scotland in the
latest quarter was **6.5%**, representing **1,481** of **22,826** first contact appointments.  

\newpage

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 2: CAMHS first contact appointments and first contact DNA rate by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>

```{r First contact DNAs table, ft.align = "left"}

flextable(table_data8, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data8)) %>%
  align(j = 2:5, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:3, width = 3, unit = "cm")  %>%
  width(j = 4:5, width = 2.5, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`

<br>

### CAMHS first contact DNA rate by age group and sex
In CAMHS, first contact DNA rates were highest for both males and females amongst 
the 'Over 15' age group in NHS Scotland between April 2025 and June 2026 at **8.7%** 
and **8.5%** respectively. Males were more likely to DNA a CAMHS first contact appointment
than females across all age groups, except in the 'Under 6' age group over the 
15-month publication period.    
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by aggregated age group and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_age_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_age_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS first contact DNA rate by SIMD and sex
Deprivation based on SIMD quintile appears to be closely associated with CAMHS first
contact appointment DNA rates across NHS Scotland. Between April 2025 and June 2026, 
females from the most deprived areas (**8.5%**) were more likely to DNA a first contact 
CAMHS appointment than those from the least deprived areas (**3.6%**). A similar trend 
was observed amongst male patients, with a first contact DNA rate of **9.5%** recorded 
amongst those from the most deprived SIMD quintile compared to **3.2%** in the least 
deprived quintile across NHS Scotland. 

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by SIMD quintile and sex reported, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_simd_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_simd_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS first contact DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex
In CAMHS, first contact appointment DNA rates for females were highest amongst
patients residing in 'very remote small towns' (**9.0%**) across NHS Scotland based
on the 8-fold urban rural classification (Figure ). For males, CAMHS first contact
appointment DNA rates were highest amongst patients living in 'very remote rural'
settings (**8.8%**). First contact DNA rates in 'large urban areas' and 
'very remote rural' settings were higher than the corresponding mean DNA rates 
for each sex across NHS Scotland. For definitions of each rurality class, please
consult the Scottish Government’s overview [documentation](https://www.gov.scot/publications/scottish-government-urban-rural-classification-2022/pages/overview/).  

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by urban rural classification and sex reported at referral, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_ur_sex_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_ur_sex_CAMHS.png"))

```    

### CAMHS first contact DNA rate by length of wait
The length of time between the referral received date and the first scheduled CAMHS
appointment date appears to have some association with the rate of non-attendance 
amongst patients. In NHS Scotland, patients that had waited between 0 to 18 weeks 
for their first scheduled CAMHS appointment had a DNA rate of 6.4% between April 2025
and June 2026. For patients that had waited between 19 to 35 weeks, the first contact
DNA rate was **8.4%**, 2 percentage points higher than those waiting 18 weeks or less. 
There does not appear to be a linear increase in first contact DNAs rates with increasing
wait beyond 35 weeks, however, with rates for patients waiting 36 to 52 weeks (**8.3%**)
or over 52 weeks (**8.5%**) for a first contact appointment similar to those for patients
waiting between 19 to 35 weeks over the publication period. The figures suggest that
the offer of contact within the 18 week referral to treatment target may be important
in a patient’s engagement with the service.   
    
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by length of wait in weeks for first contact appointment, NHS Scotland, April 2025 to June 2026 </Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_wait_length_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_wait_length_CAMHS.png"))

```

### CAMHS first contact DNA rate by geographic region

The first contact DNA rate for CAMHS has shown variability between geographic regions
over the publication period from April 2025 to June 2026. The increase in first 
contact DNA rates in the East from September 2025 onwards is likely attributable
to changes in methodologies used to extract data at board level in NHS Lothian, 
rather than reflecting a true increase in patient non-attendance. Since September
2025, first contact DNA rates across health board regions have tracked at relatively
similar rates month to month, and have been consistently below 10.0% across NHS 
Scotland (Figure) (see [Glossary](#glossary) section for more information). 

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Figure : First contact crude DNA rate by health board region, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r first_con_dnas_by_hb_chart, fig.width=7.18, fig.height=3.31, include = TRUE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("//PHI_conf/MentalHealth5/CAPTND/CAPTND_shorewise/output/analysis_", data_analysis_latest_date, "/shorewise_publication/data/appointments_firstcon/dna_hb_region_CAMHS.png"))

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. Variability in first contact DNA rates in the East region between `r date_label_5qt` and `r date_label` are likely due to NHS Lothian updating the report used for data extraction at board level. 
<br>   


## CAPTND quality assurance

<Div custom-style = "Table or chart caption">Table 3: Difference between raw count of submitted appointment and optimised count of appointments in PT by health board, April 2025 to June 2026 ^a^</Div>
```{r Total appt comparison, ft.align = "left"}

flextable(table_data6, theme_fun = NULL) %>%
  my_ft_format() %>%
  flextable::bold(i = nrow(table_data6)) %>%
  align(j = 2:4, align = "right", part = "all") %>%
  height(height = 0.236, part = "body") %>%
  hrule(rule = "atleast", part = "body") %>%
  width(j = 1, width = 4, unit = "cm") %>%
  width(j = 2:4, width = 3, unit = "cm")
  #set_caption(caption = paste0("Quarter ending ", date_label), align_with_table = TRUE)

```
<Div custom-style = "Table or chart note">
a. \ \ \ CAPTND extract `r data_analysis_latest_date`

\newpage
# Glossary  

**CAMHS**  
Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services provided by NHS Scotland. Services
are provided by teams of clinicians including psychiatrists, mental health nurses,
clinical psychologists, occupational therapists and other allied health professionals.
These services are based mainly in outpatient clinics and in the community. CAMHS
are multi-disciplinary teams that provide treatment and/or interventions for children
and young people experiencing mental health problems. Where a child or young person
is awaiting an assessment for a neurodevelopmental condition such as ASD, or ADHD,
they may not be appropriate for CAMHS. This is because they do not meet the National Referral
Criteria as described in the CAMHS national service specification and may instead
be referred onto a neurodevelopmental (ND) pathway. This release does not report
on the neurodevelopmental (ND) pathway including those children and young people
on the neurodevelopmental waiting list.

**Children and young people**  
The people served by CAMHS. Some areas provide services for all those under 18, 
while others offer services to those over 16 only if they are in full time education.

**Key variables**  
The combination of identifying variables - including 'UCPN' and 'CHI'/'UPI' -
allowing a patient pathway to be created, which enables further analysis in CAPTND.

**Mean**  
The arithmetical average value i.e. total of a number of values / the total number of 
values.

**Median**  
The middle value when the data values in a dataset are ranked in increasing (or 
decreasing) order of magnitude.

**Total appointments**  
The total number of appointments recorded in CAPTND, whether attended or not. An 
individual can have more than one appointment on a given day.

**First contact appointment**  
The first appointment date that appears in an individual's care pathway.

**DNA Rate**  
Did not attend (DNA) refers to appointments in which a patient did not attend and
gave no prior warning. The DNA rate is the proportion of appointments for which
this occurs out of all appointments in a given period. If for first contact appointments only this
is referred to as the *first contact DNA rate*.

**Region**  
The health boards that make up NHS Scotland can be divided into three geographical
regions:  
East region: *Borders, Fife, Lothian*  
North region: *Highland, Grampian, Orkney, Tayside, Western Isles, Shetland*  
West region: *Ayrshire & Arran, Greater Glasgow & Clyde, Lanarkshire, Forth Valley, Dumfries & Galloway*  

**Official statistics/Official statistics in development:**  
[Official statistics](https://code.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/glossary/#pid-official-statistics)
are statistics produced on behalf of the UK government, while [official statistics in development](https://code.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/glossary/#pid-official-statistics-in-development)
are official statistics which are undergoing a development, and are under testing
by users for quality, trustworthiness, and value.

\newpage

# Contact
**Alan Coventry, Senior information analyst**  
Public Health Scotland  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)

**Luke Taylor, Senior information analyst**  
Public Health Scotland  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)

For all media enquiries please email [phs.comms@phs.scot](mailto:phs.comms@phs.scot) or call 0131 275 6105.

# Further Information
Further information and data for this publication are available from the [publication page](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/child-and-adolescent-mental-health-services-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/) on our website.

The next release of this publication will be `r next_pub_date`.

# Rate this publication
Let us know what you think about this publication via. the link at the bottom of this [publication page](https://publichealthscotland.scot/publications/child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-captnd/child-and-adolescent-mental-health-services-in-child-adolescent-and-psychological-therapies-national-dataset-3-march-2026/) on the PHS website.

\newpage

# Appendices
## Appendix 1 - Background information

In 2019, an official letter was shared with all NHS Chief Executives and Chief
Officers. This letter stated that the focus on Mental Health (MH) continues to grow.
This brings with it increased demand for services, and a need for a robust evidence
base to support health boards and integrated joint boards (IJBs), where children’s
services are integrated, in effective planning and monitoring.  

One key component of the evidence base is the information health boards gather on adults,
children and young people who receive treatment through CAMHS or PT services. In
order to improve these data, the Scottish Government commissioned ISD (now PHS) to
work with boards to develop an individual level core dataset (CAPTND).
CAPTND data are still at an experimental stage therefore any inferences or
conclusions from this analysis must be treated with caution.  

\newpage

## Appendix 2 - Publication metadata

**Publication title**  
Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND):  
Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) Appointment 'Did Not Attend' 
(DNA) Report (April 2025 - June 2026)

**Description**  
Total and first contact appointment DNAs in Child and Adolescent Mental Health 
Services (CAMHS) recorded in the Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies
National Dataset (CAPTND), at NHS board and Scotland-level.

**Theme**  
Health and Social Care

**Topic**  
Mental health

**Format**  
Word document

**Data source(s)**  
Child, Adolescent, and Psychological Therapies National Dataset (CAPTND), 
Scottish Index of Multiple Deprivation (SIMD), 
Scottish Government Urban Rural Classification.

**Date that data are acquired**  
`r format(as.Date(data_analysis_latest_date), format = "%dth of %B %Y")`

**Release date**  
`r pub_date`

**Frequency**  
6 months

**Timeframe of data and timeliness**  
Data from `r date_label_5qt` to `r date_label`. Biannual release.

**Continuity of data**  
Information has been collected nationally since June 2019.

**Concepts and definitions**  
See the glossary and variables used sections of the report.

**Relevance and key uses of the statistics**  
Data offer detail around individuals accessing mental health services in Scotland.
However, data are still in development so uses are limited given inferences must be
made with caution.

**Accuracy**  
Data are still in development. Data quality investigations to improve accuracy are
ongoing.

**Completeness**  
Data are still in development and many data items in the dataset cannot be provided.
In some cases, missing data items can be inferred from other variables relating
to the same individual - e.g. date of birth can be gleaned from the CHI number -
which has been used where appropriate to achieve maximum completeness of data.

**Comparability**  
Comparisons are discouraged. There will be differences in the measures used and
collection methods compared with CAMHS waiting times statistics, as
well as differences in service structures between the administrations. Users must
carefully read the publications.

**Accessibility**  
It is the policy of Public Health Scotland to make its web sites and products
accessible according to published guidelines. More information on accessibility
can be found on the [PHS website](https://publichealthscotland.scot/accessibility/).

**Value type and unit of measurement**  
Number and percentage of patients by NHS board.

**Disclosure**  
The PHS protocol on Statistical Disclosure Protocol is followed.

**Official statistics accreditation**  
Official statistics in development

**Last published**  
`r prev_pub_date`

**Next published**  
`r next_pub_date`

**Date of first publication**  
1st of June 2021

**Help email**  
[phs.captnd@phs.scot](mailto:phs.captnd@phs.scot)
<br>

**Date form completed**  
`r format(Sys.time(), "%dth of %B %Y")`

\newpage

## Appendix 3 - Early access details

### Pre-release access

Under terms of the 'Pre-release Access to Official Statistics (Scotland) Order 2008',
PHS is obliged to publish information on those receiving pre-release access ('pre-release
access' refers to statistics in their final form prior to publication). The standard
maximum pre-release access is five working days. Shown below are details of those
receiving standard pre-release access.

### Standard pre-release access:

Scottish Government Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC)  
NHS board chief executives  
NHS board communication leads  

### Early access for quality assurance

These statistics will also have been made available to those who needed access to
help quality assure the publication.

\newpage

## Appendix 4 – PHS and official statistics

### About Public Health Scotland (PHS)

PHS is a knowledge-based and intelligence driven organisation with a critical
reliance on data and information to enable it to be an independent voice for the
public's health, leading collaboratively and effectively across the Scottish public
health system, accountable at local and national levels, and providing leadership
and focus for achieving better health and wellbeing outcomes for the population.
Our statistics comply with the [Code of Practice for Statistics](https://www.statisticsauthority.gov.uk/code-of-practice/the-code/)
in terms of trustworthiness, high quality and public value. This also means that
we keep data secure at all stages, through collection, processing, analysis and
output production, and adhere to the Office for National Statistics '[Five Safes](https://blog.ons.gov.uk/2017/01/27/the-five-safes-data-privacy-at-ons/)'
of data privacy.

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<Div custom-style = "*Other formats textbox (only for RMarkdown use)">
Translations and other formats are available on request at:  
phs.otherformats@phs.scot or 0131 314 5300.

This publication is licensed for re-use under the [Open Government Licence v3.0](http://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/). For more information, visit [www.publichealthscotland.scot/ogl](http://www.publichealthscotland.scot/ogl)
</Div>
